frameworks.name: bounded-memory reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: agent learns reward values under a fixed memory-capacity constraint

frameworks.name: optimal perseveration
frameworks.one_line: capacity-rational repetition bias toward frequently chosen actions

frameworks.name: computational psychiatry
frameworks.one_line: parsing anhedonia symptoms into mechanistic cognitive parameters

core_question: Can anhedonia and kappa-opioid antagonist treatment effects be dissociated as deficits versus gains in memory capacity and perseveration efficiency within a bounded-memory RL agent?
key_assumptions: memory capacity for reward values is finite and costly
rational agents perseverate optimally given their capacity
deviations from optimal perseveration constitute inefficiency
manipulations: KOR antagonist vs placebo in an RCT for anhedonia
secondary analyses across three additional reward-learning datasets
key_findings: anhedonia is associated with deficits in perseveration efficiency but not memory
KOR antagonism increases both memory capacity and efficiency
reward insensitivity in anhedonia can be linked to limited memory capacity
the two KOR cognitive effects are dissociable, only one tied to anhedonia
interpretations: anhedonia reflects suboptimal use of bounded memory rather than reduced capacity
KOR antagonism has distinct cognitive effects, only one of which maps onto anhedonia
